name: Python Core Foundations
desc: Types, slicing, control flow, collections and functions — the way working Python is actually written.

questions.card:
What: An f-string (f"...") lets you drop a variable straight into text using { }, instead of gluing pieces together with +.

Why: String concatenation with + breaks the moment a value isn't already a string (an int, for example) and gets unreadable past two or three pieces. f-strings read like the sentence you're building and convert values automatically.

How: Prefix the string literal with f, then put any expression inside { } — Python evaluates it and inserts the result.

name = "ada" greeting = f"hi, {name}" print(greeting) # hi, ada
questions.mission: Build the string hi, ada from name and store in line.

questions.card:
What: Slicing (s[start:stop]) pulls a substring out by position without a loop or a library.

Why: Fixed-width fields (dates, IDs, codes) show up constantly in real data — the first 4 characters of an ISO date are always the year, so slicing is the direct tool, no parsing needed.

How: raw[:4] means "from the start up to (not including) index 4" — indices 0,1,2,3, four characters.

raw = "2026-07-08" year = raw[:4] print(year) # 2026
questions.mission: Pull the 4-digit year out of raw into year.

questions.card:
What: str.upper() returns a new string with every letter capitalized.

Why: Case-insensitive comparisons and shouty UI labels both start by normalizing case — Python never mutates the original string (strings are immutable), so you always get a new one back.

How: Call .upper() on the string and store the result — the original variable is untouched.

code = "ab12" up = code.upper() print(up) # AB12
questions.mission: Uppercase code into up.

questions.card:
What: str.split(sep) breaks a string into a list wherever sep occurs.

Why: Raw text (a CSV row, a log line, user input) arrives as one long string — splitting on a delimiter is the first step before you can work with the individual fields.

How: row.split(",") cuts on every comma and returns a list of the pieces in order.

row = "a,b,c" parts = row.split(",") print(parts) # ['a', 'b', 'c']
questions.mission: Split row on the comma into parts.

questions.card:
What: .strip() removes leading/trailing whitespace; .lower() lowercases. Chaining both normalizes messy input in one line.

Why: User-typed text almost always carries stray spaces and inconsistent case — normalizing before comparing or storing avoids bugs like "Ada" != "ada ".

How: Methods chain left to right: s.strip() runs first, then .lower() runs on its result.

s = " HELLO " clean = s.strip().lower() print(clean) # 'hello'
questions.mission: Trim and lowercase s into clean.

questions.card:
What: str.replace(old, new) swaps every occurrence of one substring for another.

Why: Reformatting between conventions — a filesystem path into a Python import path, a US date into ISO — is almost always a targeted find-and-replace, not a full rewrite.

How: path.replace("/", ".") scans path and replaces every / with ..

path = "a/b/c" dotted = path.replace("/", ".") print(dotted) # a.b.c
questions.mission: Turn the slashes in path into dots, into dotted.

questions.card:
What: An f-string format spec ({value:.2f}) controls how a number renders — .2f means "fixed-point, 2 digits after the decimal".

Why: Raw floats print with unpredictable trailing digits (3.140000000001-style noise never happens here, but arbitrary precision does) — money, percentages and scores all need a fixed, predictable display width.

How: Put the format spec after a colon inside the braces: f"{pi:.2f}".

pi = 3.14159 txt = f"{pi:.2f}" print(txt) # 3.14
questions.mission: Format pi to 2 decimals as an f-string into txt.

questions.card:
What: sep.join(list) is the inverse of split — it glues a list of strings back together with sep between each pair.

Why: It lives on the separator, not the list ("-".join(words), not words.join("-")) — that trips up almost everyone the first time, so it's worth drilling until it's automatic.

How: Call .join() on the separator string, passing the list as the argument.

words = ["a", "b", "c"] line = "-".join(words) print(line) # a-b-c
questions.mission: Join words with "-" into line.

questions.card:
What: str.startswith(prefix) returns a bool — True if the string begins with prefix.

Why: It's the safe way to check a prefix — slicing and comparing (url[:5] == "https") breaks silently on short strings; startswith never throws.

How: Call .startswith() with the prefix you're checking for; store the resulting bool.

url = "https://x.io" secure = url.startswith("https") print(secure) # True
questions.mission: Is url HTTPS? Store the bool in secure.

questions.card:
What: A slug is the URL-safe form of a title — lowercase, spaces swapped for dashes ("My Post" → "my-post"). Here you write it as a reusable function.

Why: Every blog, wiki and e-commerce site needs this exact transform to build clean URLs — it's small enough to hand-write and common enough that you'll rewrite it from memory many times.

How: Combine what you've already used: .lower() to normalize case, then .replace(" ", "-") to swap spaces for dashes. One expression, returned directly.

def slugify(s): return s.lower().replace(" ", "-") print(slugify("Hello World")) # hello-world
questions.mission: Write slugify(s): lowercase, then replace spaces with "-". One return line.

questions.card:
What: Python evaluates *, / before +, - — the same order-of-operations rule as arithmetic on paper.

Why: Precedence bugs are silent — the code runs, it just computes the wrong thing. Knowing the order by heart (rather than guessing and adding parens defensively) is what lets you read math-heavy code at a glance.

How: 2 + 3 * 4 multiplies 3 * 4 first (12), then adds 2 — not (2 + 3) * 4.

r = 2 + 3 * 4 print(r) # 14, not 20
questions.mission: Compute 2 + 3 * 4 into r (write the expression).

questions.card:
What: // divides and rounds down to the nearest whole number — "how many whole times does this fit".

Why: Regular division (/) always returns a float, even for exact divisions — floor division is what you reach for whenever the answer has to be a count of whole units (weeks, pages, batches).

How: 100 // 7 is 14 (7 fits into 100 fourteen whole times, with 2 left over — that remainder is what % gives you).

weeks = 100 // 7 print(weeks) # 14
questions.mission: Whole weeks in 100 days into weeks.

questions.card:
What: % is the modulo operator — it returns the remainder of a division. A number is even exactly when n % 2 == 0.

Why: This is the standard idiom for even/odd, and modulo more generally is how you detect cycles: every Nth item, wraparound indices, clock arithmetic.

How: 42 % 2 is 0 (42 divides evenly by 2), and comparing that to 0 gives a bool directly — no if needed.

even = 42 % 2 == 0 print(even) # True
questions.mission: Store the bool "42 is even" in even.

questions.card:
What: ** is exponentiation — base ** exponent.

Why: It shows up everywhere quantitative: compounding, byte sizes (2**10 == 1024), polynomial terms, ML loss functions squaring an error.

How: 2 ** 10 multiplies 2 by itself 10 times: 1024.

p = 2 ** 10 print(p) # 1024
questions.mission: 2 to the 10th into p.

questions.card:
What: round(value, ndigits) rounds to a given number of decimal places and returns a float.

Why: Display and storage often need fewer digits than a computation naturally produces — rounding for a UI is a different concern from formatting for display (round() gives you back a real number you can still do math with; an f-string spec only changes how it prints).

How: round(x, 2) keeps 2 digits after the decimal point, rounding the third digit normally.

x = 3.14159 r = round(x, 2) print(r) # 3.14
questions.mission: Round x to 2 decimals into r.

questions.card:
What: abs(n) strips the sign — it returns the distance from zero, always non-negative.

Why: Differences and distances (error magnitude, offsets, deltas between two frames of data) care about "how far", not direction — abs() is the direct way to say that instead of an if n < 0 branch.

How: abs(-7) returns 7; abs(7) also returns 7.

a = -7 d = abs(a) print(d) # 7
questions.mission: Absolute value of a into d.

questions.card:
What: Python lets you chain comparisons: a <= x < b means "x is at least a AND strictly less than b" — one readable expression instead of two joined by and.

Why: Range checks (valid index, percentage bounds, a value inside a threshold window) are extremely common, and the chained form matches how you'd say it out loud: "between 0 and 100".

How: 0 <= x < 100 is equivalent to 0 <= x and x < 100, evaluated left to right.

x = 57 valid = 0 <= x < 100 print(valid) # True
questions.mission: Bool "x is in [0, 100)" into valid.

questions.card:
What: Python's conditional expression — a if condition else b — picks one of two values inline, without a multi-line if/else.

Why: When you're assigning one of two values based on a condition, a full if/else block is 4 lines of ceremony for what is really one decision — the ternary form keeps the assignment on one line.

How: Read it right-to-left-ish: "\"even\" if n % 2 == 0, otherwise \"odd\"".

n = 4 label = "even" if n % 2 == 0 else "odd" print(label) # even
questions.mission: Set label to "even" when n is even, else "odd".

questions.card:
What: and requires both sides to be true; or requires at least one. They combine bools the same way as everyday logic.

Why: Access rules, feature gates and validation almost always combine more than one condition — "adult AND a member" is a single rule made of two checks.

How: age >= 18 evaluates to a bool, member already is one, and and combines them.

age = 20 member = True allowed = age >= 18 and member print(allowed) # True
questions.mission: Bool "adult and member" into allowed.

questions.card:
What: The Gregorian leap-year rule: divisible by 4, UNLESS divisible by 100, UNLESS ALSO divisible by 400. Here you encode that as a function that returns a bool.

Why: It's the canonical example of a rule that looks like one condition but is actually three nested exceptions — a good forcing function for combining and/or correctly instead of getting a plausible-looking wrong answer (e.g. missing the 1900-is-NOT-a-leap-year case).

How: y % 4 == 0 catches the common case; (y % 100 != 0 or y % 400 == 0) excludes century years except the ones divisible by 400.

def is_leap(y): return y % 4 == 0 and (y % 100 != 0 or y % 400 == 0) print(is_leap(2000)) # True print(is_leap(1900)) # False
questions.mission: Write is_leap(y) returning the bool. One return line.
questions.sol:
def is_leap(y):
    return y % 4 == 0 and (y % 100 != 0 or y % 400 == 0)

questions.card:
What: if / elif / else runs the FIRST branch whose condition is true, then skips the rest — order matters.

Why: Grading, tiered pricing, HTTP status handling — anywhere thresholds decide behavior, and the thresholds overlap conceptually (a 95 is also >= 80), so ordering from most to least specific is what makes the logic correct.

How: Check the highest bar first (>= 90). If it fails, elif checks the next bar. else is whatever's left.

def grade(score): if score >= 90: return "A" elif score >= 80: return "B" else: return "C" print(grade(95)) # A
questions.mission: Write grade(score): "A" ≥90, "B" ≥80, else "C".

questions.card:
What: A while loop repeats its body as long as its condition stays true — you control both the exit condition and the step manually.

Why: It's the general-purpose loop: use it when the number of iterations isn't known up front, or when the step logic is more than "go from a to b" (which is what for/range is for instead).

How: Start total and i at their initial values, then on every pass add i to total and increment i — stop once i passes n.

n = 5 total = 0 i = 1 while i <= n: total += i i += 1 print(total) # 15
questions.mission: Sum 1..n with a while loop into total (init total=0, i=1).
questions.sol:
total = 0
i = 1
while i <= n:
    total += i
    i += 1

questions.card:
What: range(1, n + 1) generates the integers 1..n; sum(...) adds them all up. Same result as the while-loop version, one line.

Why: Once a loop is just "accumulate over a known range", Python's built-ins (sum, range) do it faster and more clearly than a hand-rolled loop — knowing when to reach for a built-in instead of writing the loop yourself is a core skill.

How: range(1, n + 1) stops BEFORE its second argument, so + 1 is what includes n itself.

n = 5 total = sum(range(1, n + 1)) print(total) # 15
questions.mission: Sum 1..n in one line into total.

questions.card:
What: A for loop over a list visits each element in order; an if inside it filters which ones count.

Why: "Count how many satisfy X" is one of the most common loop shapes there is — the same pattern (init a counter, loop, conditionally increment) reappears constantly, just with the condition swapped out.

How: Start count at 0. For each x in nums, if x > 0, add 1 to count.

nums = [3, -1, 4, -2] count = 0 for x in nums: if x > 0: count += 1 print(count) # 2
questions.mission: Count positive numbers in nums into count (loop).

questions.card:
What: enumerate(list) yields (index, value) pairs — it pairs each item with its position without you tracking a counter by hand.

Why: Manually maintaining an index (i = 0, then i += 1 at the end of the loop body) is a classic off-by-one source — enumerate removes the whole failure mode.

How: list(enumerate(nums)) turns the lazy enumerate object into a concrete list of tuples you can inspect.

nums = [3, -1, 4] pairs = list(enumerate(nums)) print(pairs) # [(0, 3), (1, -1), (2, 4)]
questions.mission: Pair each num with its index into pairs.

questions.card:
What: zip(a, b) pairs up elements from two sequences position-by-position — first with first, second with second.

Why: Whenever two parallel lists represent the same underlying rows (names and scores, keys and values, x's and y's for a plot) zip is how you walk them together instead of indexing both by hand.

How: list(zip(a, b)) stops at the shorter of the two inputs, producing one tuple per position.

a = [1, 2] b = ["x", "y"] pairs = list(zip(a, b)) print(pairs) # [(1, 'x'), (2, 'y')]
questions.mission: Pair a with b into pairs.

questions.card:
What: break exits a loop immediately — execution jumps straight past it, skipping any remaining iterations.

Why: Searching for "the first thing that matches" shouldn't keep scanning after it's found — on a large list that's wasted work, and semantically the loop's job is already done.

How: Initialize found to None. Loop through nums; the moment one is even, store it in found and break.

nums = [1, 3, 4, 7] found = None for x in nums: if x % 2 == 0: found = x break print(found) # 4
questions.mission: Find the first even in nums into found (init None, break).
questions.ph: found = None / for x in nums: ...

questions.card:
What: continue skips the rest of the current iteration's body and moves straight to the next one — it doesn't exit the loop like break.

Why: It reads better than wrapping the whole loop body in an if — "skip this one and move on" is a guard clause, and guard clauses keep loop bodies flat instead of nested.

How: For each x, if it's odd, continue (skip straight to the next x). Only even values reach the total += x line.

nums = [1, 2, 3, 4] total = 0 for x in nums: if x % 2 != 0: continue total += x print(total) # 6
questions.mission: Sum only the evens of nums into total, skipping odds with continue.

questions.card:
What: A list comprehension — [expr for item in iterable] — builds a new list from an existing one in a single expression.

Why: It replaces the extremely common 3-line pattern (make an empty list, loop, append) with one line that's just as readable once it's familiar — it's the idiomatic Python way to transform a sequence.

How: x * x for x in range(5) reads as "the square of x, for every x in 0..4".

squares = [x * x for x in range(5)] print(squares) # [0, 1, 4, 9, 16]
questions.mission: Squares of 0..4 into squares.
questions.ph: squares = [x * x for x in range(5)]
questions.sol: squares = [x * x for x in range(5)]

questions.card:
What: A dict comprehension — {key_expr: value_expr for item in iterable} — builds a dict in one expression, the same idea as a list comprehension but producing key/value pairs.

Why: Any time you're mapping each item in a collection to a derived value and need to look it up later by the original item (word → its length, id → its record), a dict comprehension builds that lookup table directly.

How: w: len(w) for w in words reads as "for every word w, map w to its length".

words = ["hi", "abc"] lengths = {w: len(w) for w in words} print(lengths) # {'hi': 2, 'abc': 3}
questions.mission: Map each word to its length into lengths.
questions.ph: lengths = {w: len(w) for w in words}
questions.sol: lengths = {w: len(w) for w in words}

questions.card:
What: The same while pattern as before, applied to counting down instead of up — the loop runs until the condition (fuel > 0) goes false.

Why: Countdown-to-zero is the shape behind timers, retry budgets and resource draining — get the exit condition or the step direction wrong and you get an infinite loop instead of a bug that just gives a wrong number.

How: Loop while fuel > 0, decrementing by 1 each pass, until it reaches exactly 0.

fuel = 5 while fuel > 0: fuel -= 1 print(fuel) # 0
questions.mission: Decrement fuel to 0 using while. Body: fuel -= 1.

questions.card:
What: Combining if and break: scan a list, and the moment a specific value is seen, stop looping.

Why: This is break in its rawest form — no accumulator, just "stop as soon as you see it" — the building block behind search loops before you learn library functions like in or .index() that do the same thing internally.

How: Loop through nums; when x == 7, break immediately — the loop variable x is left holding the matched value.

nums = [1, 3, 7, 9] for x in nums: if x == 7: break print(x) # 7 (loop stopped here, never reached 9)
questions.mission: Find the first x in nums equal to 7 and break.

questions.card:
What: Another continue drill, this time printing instead of accumulating — the skip happens before the print line, so evens never reach it.

Why: Filtering what gets printed/logged/sent is a direct real-world use of continue — e.g. skipping already-processed records in a batch job.

How: For each x, if it's even, continue (skip the print). Odd values fall through to print(x).

nums = [1, 2, 3, 4] for x in nums: if x % 2 == 0: continue print(x) # 1 # 3
questions.mission: Skip even x, print odd ones.

questions.card:
What: Unpacking enumerate directly in the for header — for i, n in enumerate(names) — gives you both the index and the value as separate loop variables, no list() or manual indexing needed.

Why: This is the form you'll actually type in real code far more often than list(enumerate(...)) — printing/logging with a position label is a constant need (numbered output, progress lines, ranked results).

How: Each iteration unpacks the (index, value) tuple straight into i and n.

names = ['ada', 'linus'] for i, n in enumerate(names): print(f'{i}:{n}') # 0:ada # 1:linus
questions.mission: Loop indices+values of names, print f'{i}:{n}'.
questions.ph:
for i, n in enumerate(names):
    print(f'{i}:{n}')
questions.sol:
for i, n in enumerate(names):
    print(f'{i}:{n}')

questions.card:
What: dict(zip(keys, vals)) builds a dict in one line by pairing two parallel lists and feeding the pairs straight to dict().

Why: It's the standard idiom for turning "two columns" data (headers + a row, keys + values from two separate API fields) into a lookup structure, without a manual loop.

How: zip(keys, vals) produces (key, value) tuples; dict() consumes exactly that shape to build the mapping.

keys = ['a', 'b'] vals = [1, 2] d = dict(zip(keys, vals)) print(d) # {'a': 1, 'b': 2}
questions.mission: Pair keys+vals into d via dict(...).

questions.card:
What: sorted(list, key=fn) sorts by whatever fn returns for each element, instead of the elements themselves — key=len sorts strings by their length.

Why: Sorting by a derived property (length, a dict field, negative value for descending) is the norm, not the exception — key is what makes sorted flexible enough for that without writing a custom comparator.

How: sorted calls len(word) for each word to get its sort key, then orders the original words by those keys — ties keep their original relative order (a stable sort).

words = ['nova', 'ada', 'hi'] bylen = sorted(words, key=len) print(bylen) # ['hi', 'ada', 'nova']
questions.mission: Sort words by length into bylen.

questions.card:
What: list.append(x) grows a list in place by adding x to the end — the classic "build a result list inside a loop" pattern.

Why: Before comprehensions feel natural, this 3-step shape (empty list → loop → append) is how you build up any result incrementally, and it's still the right tool once the loop body has more than one line.

How: Start with out = []. Each pass through range(5), compute x * x and append it.

out = [] for x in range(5): out.append(x * x) print(out) # [0, 1, 4, 9, 16]
questions.mission: Build squares 0..4 by appending in a loop into out.
questions.ph: out = [] / for x in range(5): out.append(...)

questions.card:
What: Slicing with a step of -1 (list[::-1]) walks the list backwards, producing a reversed copy.

Why: It's the fastest way to reverse a sequence, and the same [::-1] trick works identically on strings and tuples — one pattern, three types.

How: [::-1] means "every element, step -1" — start at the end, walk to the beginning.

items = [1, 2, 3] rev = items[::-1] print(rev) # [3, 2, 1]
questions.mission: Reverse items into rev.

questions.card:
What: Same key= idea as before, this time on a fresh example to lock it in: sort a list of words by their length rather than alphabetically.

Why: Sorting by a derived value instead of the raw value is a pattern you'll reuse constantly (sort users by signup date, products by price) — it's worth seeing it a second time with different data.

How: sorted(words, key=len) orders words by the result of calling len() on each one.

words = ['ccc', 'a', 'bb'] ordered = sorted(words, key=len) print(ordered) # ['a', 'bb', 'ccc']
questions.mission: Sort words by length into ordered.

questions.card:
What: dict.get(key, default) looks up key and returns default instead of raising KeyError when it's missing.

Why: Config objects, JSON payloads and optional fields are never guaranteed to have every key — .get() with a default is the standard way to read "this or a sensible fallback" in one line, instead of a try/except.

How: cfg.get("color", "blue") returns cfg["color"] if present, otherwise "blue".

cfg = {} color = cfg.get("color", "blue") print(color) # blue
questions.mission: Read "color" from cfg, default "blue", into color.

questions.card:
What: dict.items() yields (key, value) pairs so you can loop over both at once.

Why: Aggregating a dict's values (totals, counts, averages) is one of the most common dict operations — .items() is how you get at both halves without a separate .keys()/lookup dance.

How: For each k, v pair, add v to the running total — the keys aren't needed here, just the values.

scores = {"a": 3, "b": 5} total = 0 for k, v in scores.items(): total += v print(total) # 8
questions.mission: Sum all values in scores into total (loop).
questions.ph: total = 0 / for k, v in scores.items(): ...

questions.card:
What: A set holds unique, unordered values. & between two sets returns their intersection — elements present in both.

Why: Sets are the right tool the moment order and duplicates stop mattering and the question becomes "which elements do these two collections share" — computing that with lists directly means nested loops; sets do it natively.

How: set(a) & set(b) converts both lists to sets first, then & keeps only the shared elements.

a = [1, 2, 3] b = [2, 3, 4] both = set(a) & set(b) print(both) # {2, 3}
questions.mission: Elements in both a and b into both.

questions.card:
What: Python can swap two variables in a single line with tuple assignment: a, b = b, a. The right side builds a tuple (b, a) before anything is reassigned, so no temporary variable is needed.

Why: In most languages a swap needs a third temp variable — Python's tuple unpacking makes the classic 3-line swap unnecessary, and the same unpacking mechanism is what powers multiple return values from functions.

How: The whole right-hand side is evaluated first (as a tuple), then unpacked into a and b — so both swap simultaneously, not one at a time.

a = 1 b = 2 a, b = b, a print(a, b) # 2 1
questions.mission: Swap a and b in one line.

questions.card:
What: A list of lists (a "grid") is indexed twice: grid[row][col] — first pick the row, then pick the column within it.

Why: Anything two-dimensional (a game board, a small matrix, pixel rows) is naturally represented as nested lists before you reach for a real array library — nested indexing is how you read a specific cell.

How: grid[1] gets the second row ([3, 4]); [1] again gets the second element of THAT row.

grid = [[1, 2], [3, 4]] v = grid[1][1] print(v) # 4
questions.mission: Grab the bottom-right of grid into v.

questions.card:
What: A comprehension can add a trailing if to keep only elements that pass a test: [expr for item in iterable if condition].

Why: Filtering-and-transforming in one pass is such a common combo that Python builds it into the comprehension syntax directly, rather than needing a separate filter() call chained with map().

How: for x in nums if x % 2 == 0 only lets even x values through to be collected.

nums = [1, 2, 3, 4] evens = [x for x in nums if x % 2 == 0] print(evens) # [2, 4]
questions.mission: Keep the evens of nums into evens.
questions.ph: evens = [x for x in nums if ...]
questions.sol: evens = [x for x in nums if x % 2 == 0]

questions.card:
What: Counting occurrences by building a dict as you loop — counts.get(w, 0) + 1 reads "how many so far, defaulting to 0 if it's new" and adds one.

Why: This exact pattern (a running dict of counts) is the basis of frequency analysis, histograms and the first step of most text-processing pipelines, before you ever reach for collections.Counter.

How: For each word, look up its current count (0 if not seen), add 1, and store it back under the same key.

words = ["a", "b", "a"] counts = {} for w in words: counts[w] = counts.get(w, 0) + 1 print(counts) # {'a': 2, 'b': 1}
questions.mission: Count occurrences of each word in words into counts.

questions.card:
What: None is Python's explicit "no value" — a distinct object, not zero, not an empty string, not false.

Why: Functions that might not have an answer (a search that found nothing, an optional field never set) return None rather than a fake placeholder value — code that checks for it can tell "absent" apart from "legitimately zero/empty".

How: Assign None directly — no quotes, no parentheses, it's a keyword-like singleton.

result = None print(result) # None
questions.mission: Assign result to None.

questions.card:
What: == checks value equality; is checks identity (same object). For None specifically, is None is the correct and idiomatic check.

Why: Because None is a true singleton (there's only ever one), is is both faster and more correct than == here — and it's a real style rule enforced by linters, not just a preference.

How: x is None returns True only when x is literally the None object — 0 and "" are NOT None, even though both are falsy.

x = None empty = x is None print(empty) # True
questions.mission: Test whether x is None into empty.

questions.card:
What: str.split() with no arguments splits on ANY whitespace (spaces, tabs, newlines) and collapses runs of it — different from split(","), which splits on that exact character only.

Why: Free-form text (a sentence, a command line, a search query) is separated by whitespace of unpredictable width — the argument-less form is what handles that without you first having to normalize the spacing.

How: line.split() breaks on whitespace and discards it, returning just the words.

line = 'hi ada' parts = line.split() print(parts) # ['hi', 'ada']
questions.mission: Split line into parts.

questions.card:
What: A second look at ",".join(list) — the inverse of split — this time framed as building CSV output rather than just gluing words.

Why: Writing data back out (to a file, an API body, a log line) is exactly as common as parsing it in — join is the half of the round-trip that's easy to forget lives on the separator, not the list.

How: ",".join(items) places a comma between every pair of adjacent items, with none before the first or after the last.

items = ['a', 'b', 'c'] csv = ','.join(items) print(csv) # a,b,c
questions.mission: Join items with "," into csv.

questions.card:
What: One more pass at .strip() alone (no .lower() this time) — trimming only the leading/trailing whitespace, leaving internal spacing and case untouched.

Why: It's worth isolating from the earlier "clean + lowercase" example: .strip() alone is what you reach for when case matters but stray edge-whitespace from user input or file reads doesn't.

How: raw.strip() removes whitespace from both ends only — anything in the middle stays exactly as typed.

raw = ' ada ' clean = raw.strip() print(clean) # 'ada'
questions.mission: Clean raw into clean.

questions.card:
What: def defines a reusable block of code with a name and parameters; return sends a value back to whoever called it.

Why: Functions are how you stop repeating yourself — write the logic once, name it, and call it wherever you need that computation instead of copy-pasting the body.

How: square(n) takes one parameter n and returns n * n — calling square(6) runs the body with n bound to 6.

def square(n): return n * n print(square(6)) # 36
questions.mission: Write square(n) returning n².

questions.card:
What: A parameter can have a default value (greeting="hi") — callers can omit it and get the default, or pass their own to override it.

Why: Defaults let a function have a sensible "usual" behavior while staying flexible — you don't need two versions of the same function, one with the extra parameter and one without.

How: greet("ada") uses the default "hi"; greet("ada", "hey") overrides it with the second positional argument.

def greet(name, greeting="hi"): return f"{greeting}, {name}" print(greet("ada")) # hi, ada print(greet("ada", "hey")) # hey, ada
questions.mission: Write greet(name, greeting="hi") returning "{greeting}, {name}".

questions.card:
What: *nums in a parameter list collects any number of positional arguments into a tuple named nums inside the function.

Why: It's what lets a function accept a flexible number of inputs — print(), max() and sum()-like helpers all work this way rather than forcing a fixed parameter count.

How: Calling total(1, 2, 3) packs (1, 2, 3) into nums; sum(nums) adds them up. Zero arguments gives an empty tuple, and sum(()) is 0.

def total(*nums): return sum(nums) print(total(1, 2, 3)) # 6 print(total()) # 0
questions.mission: Write total(*nums) returning their sum.

questions.card:
What: **opts in a parameter list collects any number of keyword arguments into a dict named opts.

Why: It's how functions accept an open-ended set of named options (think config overrides, or how dict(a=1, b=2) itself works) without listing every possible parameter up front.

How: build(x=1, y=2) packs {"x": 1, "y": 2} into opts; len(opts) counts how many were passed.

def build(**opts): return len(opts) print(build(x=1, y=2)) # 2 print(build()) # 0
questions.mission: Write build(**opts) returning how many options were passed.

questions.card:
What: lambda writes a small, unnamed function inline — lambda p: p[1] is a function that takes p and returns its second item.

Why: For a one-off function that's only ever used as an argument (like a sort key), defining it with def elsewhere and naming it is more ceremony than the logic deserves — lambda keeps it inline, right where it's used.

How: sorted(pairs, key=lambda p: p[1]) sorts each tuple p by its second element, ascending.

pairs = [("a", 3), ("b", 1)] ordered = sorted(pairs, key=lambda p: p[1]) print(ordered) # [('b', 1), ('a', 3)]
questions.mission: Sort pairs by their second item into ordered.

questions.card:
What: map(fn, iterable) applies fn to every element and returns a lazy iterator of the results — wrap it in list() to see them.

Why: It's the functional-programming counterpart to a list comprehension — same result as [x * 2 for x in nums], useful when you already have a function (named or lambda) you want to apply directly without writing a comprehension.

How: map(lambda x: x * 2, nums) doubles every element; list(...) forces it into a concrete list.

nums = [1, 2, 3] doubled = list(map(lambda x: x * 2, nums)) print(doubled) # [2, 4, 6]
questions.mission: Double every number in nums into doubled.

questions.card:
What: A recursive function calls itself with a smaller version of the problem, until it hits a base case simple enough to answer directly without recursing further.

Why: Some problems (factorial, tree/nested-structure traversal, divide-and-conquer algorithms) are naturally defined in terms of themselves — recursion lets the code mirror that definition directly instead of simulating it with an explicit stack.

How: Base case: n <= 1 returns 1 directly. Otherwise, return n * factorial(n - 1) — each call is a strictly smaller problem, so it must eventually hit the base case.

def factorial(n): if n <= 1: return 1 return n * factorial(n - 1) print(factorial(5)) # 120
questions.mission: Write recursive factorial(n) (base: n ≤ 1 → 1).

questions.card:
What: A higher-order function takes another function as an argument (or returns one) — apply(fn, x) doesn't know or care what fn does, it just calls it.

Why: This is the mechanism behind sorted(..., key=...), map(), event handlers and callbacks generally — functions being ordinary values you can pass around is one of Python's most load-bearing features.

How: apply(fn, x) simply calls fn(x) and returns whatever that produces — fn can be a lambda, a named function, or even a builtin like str.

def apply(fn, x): return fn(x) print(apply(lambda x: x + 1, 5)) # 6
questions.mission: Write apply(fn, x) returning fn(x).

questions.card:
What: *nums at a CALL site does the opposite of *nums in a definition — it spreads a list back out into separate positional arguments.

Why: It's how you forward a list of values to a function that takes individual arguments (or a *args function, as here) without manually indexing into it — fn(*my_list) instead of fn(my_list[0], my_list[1], ...).

How: total(*nums) with nums = [1, 2, 3] calls exactly like total(1, 2, 3).

def total(*nums): return sum(nums) nums = [1, 2, 3] r = total(*nums) print(r) # 6
questions.mission: Call total with nums spread out, into r.

questions.card:
What: A two-step function: compute an intermediate value (m = max(nums)), then use it to build the final result — normalizing a list so its largest value becomes 1.0.

Why: Min-max normalization like this is the first thing you do before feeding numbers into almost any ML model — features on wildly different scales (age vs. income) otherwise dominate the ones with bigger raw numbers for no meaningful reason.

How: Find the max once, store it, then divide every element by that same max inside a comprehension — computing max(nums) fresh inside the loop would work but re-scans the list every iteration.

def normalize(nums): m = max(nums) return [x / m for x in nums] print(normalize([1, 2, 4])) # [0.25, 0.5, 1.0]
questions.mission: Write normalize(nums): divide each value by max(nums). Two lines.

questions.card:
What: The same *args pattern as before, drilled again with the conventional parameter name args (the name Python's own docs and error messages use).

Why: You'll see *args (and **kwargs) constantly in library code and decorators, specifically under these exact names — recognizing the convention matters as much as understanding the mechanism.

How: *args collects all positional arguments into a tuple called args; sum(args) adds them.

def total(*args): return sum(args) print(total(1, 2, 3)) # 6
questions.mission: Define total(*args) returning sum(args).

questions.card:
What: Same idea as build(**opts) earlier, but this time returning the whole collected dict instead of just its length — so you can see exactly what **kwargs gives you.

Why: Returning (or forwarding) **kwargs unchanged is extremely common in wrapper functions and decorators that pass options through to another function without needing to know each one by name.

How: show(a=1, b=2) packs the keyword arguments into kw as {"a": 1, "b": 2}, which is returned as-is.

def show(**kw): return kw print(show(a=1, b=2)) # {'a': 1, 'b': 2}
questions.mission: Define show(**kw) returning kw.

questions.card:
What: A bare * in a parameter list marks everything after it as keyword-only — k can only be passed as k=value, never positionally.

Why: It's a readability guardrail library authors use deliberately: for a function with several similar-looking parameters, forcing some to be named at the call site (f(1, k=2) instead of the ambiguous f(1, 2)) prevents mixing them up by position.

How: x can be positional or keyword; k, appearing after the bare *, must always be passed by name.

def f(x, *, k): return x + k print(f(1, k=2)) # 3
questions.mission: Define f(x, *, k) returning x + k.
questions.ph:
def f(x, *, k):
    return x + k
questions.sol:
def f(x, *, k):
    return x + k

questions.card:
What: The same factorial recursion as before, written with a ternary instead of an if/else block — one expression: 1 if n == 0 else n * fact(n - 1).

Why: Seeing the identical logic in two syntactic shapes (block-style, then ternary) is what makes the ternary form click as "just another way to write a return value", not a different concept.

How: When n == 0, return 1 directly (the base case). Otherwise return n * fact(n - 1) — the recursive step.

def fact(n): return 1 if n == 0 else n * fact(n - 1) print(fact(5)) # 120
questions.mission: Define fact(n) returning 1 at 0 else n*fact(n-1).
questions.ph:
def fact(n):
    return 1 if n == 0 else n * fact(n - 1)
questions.sol:
def fact(n):
    return 1 if n == 0 else n * fact(n - 1)

questions.card:
What: import math loads Python's built-in math module, giving you access to math.sqrt, math.floor, math.pi and more under the math. prefix.

Why: The standard library ships with this instead of every project reimplementing square roots and trig — importing it is the first line of nearly every numeric Python script, and it's the direct predecessor to importing third-party libraries like NumPy the same way.

How: Just the one line — no from, no aliasing — then anything in the module is reachable as math.<name>.

import math print(math.sqrt(16)) # 4.0 print(math.floor(9.7)) # 9
questions.mission: Import the standard math module.
